1.description: Outperformance on HML Returns: Modified over As-Reported Book Value Notes. (a) Excess HML returns by decade. (b) Excess HML returns by Fama and French 12 industries. (c) Excess HML returns by Fama and French 12 industries (big firms). (d) Excess HML returns by Fama and French 12 industries (small firms). We follow Fama and French (1992 , 1993 ) and Lev and Srivastava (2022) to calculate HML returns based on industry-adjusted portfolios. Firms are classified into value and glamour categories after dividing them into large and small categories. Portfolios with the highest (lowest) 30% market-to-book ratio, ranked by industry, are called glamour (value) stocks. HML returns are computed as one half the returns from going long on large and small value portfolios and one half the returns from shorting large and small glamour portfolios. Portfolio returns are calculated by weighting returns of constituent stocks by market value as of June 30, the date of portfolio formation. Monthly returns in calendar year t are for portfolios formed on June 30, t − 1 (from January to June), and for portfolios formed on June 30, t (from July to December). We calculate HML returns with as-reported book values and modified book values based on our method. The procedure for calculating modified book values with the capitalization of in-house intangibles with industry-specific capitalization and amortization rates is described in Section 2 . Positive excess HML returns indicate outperformance of modified HML returns (that is, modified HML returns are greater than as-reported HML returns) and negative excess HML returns indicate underperformance of modified HML returns. Panel (a) shows the excess HML returns by decade for all firms and by dividing the sample into big and small firms. For ease of exposition, we include data from years 2021 and 2022 in the 2011–2022 decade. Panel (b) shows the excess HML returns by Fama and French 12 industry sectors. Panels (c) and (d) present panel (b) results for big and small firms, respectively.

2.description: Outperformance on HML Returns: Modified over Mechanically Adjusted Book Value Notes. We follow Fama and French (1992 , 1993 ) and Lev and Srivastava (2022) to calculate HML returns based on industry-adjusted portfolios. Firms are classified into value and glamour categories after dividing them into large and small categories. Portfolios with the highest (lowest) 30% market-to-book ratio, ranked by industry, are called glamour (value) stocks. HML returns are computed as one half the returns from going long on large and small value portfolios and one half the returns from shorting large and small glamour portfolios. Portfolio returns are calculated by weighting returns of constituent stocks by market value as of June 30, the date of portfolio formation. Monthly returns in calendar year t are for portfolios formed on June 30, t − 1 (from January to June), and for portfolios formed on June 30, t (from July to December). We calculate HML returns with modified book values based on our method and mechanically adjusted book values. Mechanically adjusted book values are based on uniform capitalization and amortization rates across industries. The procedure for calculating modified book values with the capitalization of in-house intangibles with industry-specific capitalization and amortization rates is described in Section 2 . Positive excess HML returns indicate outperformance of modified HML returns (that is, modified HML returns are greater than mechanically adjusted HML returns) and negative excess HML returns indicate underperformance of modified HML returns. The figure shows the excess HML returns by decade for all firms and by dividing the sample into big and small firms. For ease of exposition, we include data from years 2021 and 2022 in the 2011–2022 decade.
